title: Chapter 32: The Forensic Brand
content: The email arrived at 9:14 AM with the subject line: **NOTICE OF EXPANDED CLAIM — Chen v. Wu / Serene Wellness Holdings, LLC**.
Chloe read it three times. Then she closed the laptop, opened it, and read it again, because surely she'd hallucinated part of it. She hadn't.
Debra Wu wasn't just claiming the TikTok revenue anymore.
She was claiming the *brand ecosystem*.

The legal language was dense—the kind of dense that required three clicks through a dictionary website and a growing sense of dread—but the core argument was simple, and simple was worse, because simple was persuasive:

The #CapybaraMethod community, its aesthetic, its language, its entire *vibe* of "no thoughts, just fluff," derived directly from Chloe's public relations training at Serene Wellness. That training was proprietary. The methods Chloe used to build her following—the content calendars, the engagement frameworks, the brand voice guidelines—were developed on company time, using company resources, under the terms of her employment contract. Therefore, anything built with those methods belonged to Serene Wellness. Therefore, the *community itself*—the hashtags, the aesthetic, the shared language of radical chill—was derivative intellectual property.
Debra Wu was claiming ownership of a vibe.
Chloe stared at the closed laptop like it might bite her.
By noon, the internet had the filing.
By two, #FreeCapybara was trending.
By four, it was the number one topic on Twitter, which was still called Twitter by everyone except the man who'd renamed it, and the discourse was exactly as unhinged as you'd expect.
**@skincaregenius**: Debra Wu really said "I own relaxation" and expected us to be normal about it
**@lawboy99**: Genuine legal question: can you copyright an *aesthetic*? Like, is "calm" IP now? Asking for every meditation app ever
**@capybaragirl**: #FreeCapybara SHE IS A LITERAL CAPYBARA AND THEY ARE TRYING TO OWN HER. THIS IS NOT A DRILL.
**@decolonize_wellness**: Serene Wellness has always been about extracting from communities and repackaging it as "mindfulness." This is just the mask off.
**@chads_rules**: Wait so if I post a chill video I owe this lady money? Lmao no

**@jordan.replies**: (This was Jordan's burner, and they were FURIOUS, posting threads longer than their actual organizing documents, breaking down every clause of the filing with a rage that was technically accurate but also very clearly a person who had not slept in thirty-six hours)

The clip that really broke things open was the interview. Debra Wu, in a cream-colored studio, cross-legged on an ivory pouf, wearing the kind of soft neutral knitwear that said "I am approachable" and "I charge $800 an hour" at the same time.
The host—it was one of those morning shows that tried to be empathetic but was really just content-shaped—leaned in with practiced concern.
"Debra, there are people online saying you're trying to *own* a community. That you're claiming the concept of being calm as your intellectual property. What do you say to that?"
Debra smiled. It was a good smile—practiced, but good. Warm. The smile of someone who'd done a lot of media training, possibly at a company she herself had founded.

"I care about Chloe," she said. "She was my best employee. I mentored her personally. This isn't about punishing her—it's about protecting our intellectual property. The systems she used to build her platform were developed at Serene Wellness. They belong to the company. And when IP is used without authorization, we have a responsibility to our stakeholders to address it."
"Even if the IP is... a feeling?"
Debra laughed softly. The kind of laugh that said, *I understand why you'd think that, and isn't that quaint.*

"The feeling isn't the IP," she said. "The *framework* is. The methodology. The brand architecture. Those are quantifiable business assets with documented development histories. Chloe is a wonderful person—and I want to make that clear, I have nothing but affection for her—but affection doesn't negate intellectual property law."
She said "affection" like she'd rehearsed it in a mirror. She probably had.
The comments were, predictably, a war crime.

Dave found Chloe under the blanket on the couch—which was, honestly, her default location at this point, and he was starting to worry that the couch was becoming a permanent feature of her identity, like a hermit crab and its shell.
He sat on the edge and pulled the blanket down just enough to see her face. One dark eye blinked at him. He held up his phone. The Debra Wu interview was still playing on loop.
"I've seen it," Chloe hummed. Low. Flat. The capybara equivalent of a sigh.
"The lawyer called," Dave said. "While you were, uh, under there."
Chloe emerged slightly. The blanket stayed around her shoulders like a cape. A very discouraged capybara cape.
"What'd they say?"

Dave's face did something he probably didn't intend it to do—it crumpled, just for a second, before he got control of it. He was not a crumple person. He was a sound engineer. He mixed things. He kept levels balanced. He did not crumple.
"Fighting it would cost fifty K minimum," he said. "Probably more, because they're claiming expanded damages now, which means more motions, more discovery, more everything. And settling—"
"I know what settling means."
A percentage of all future earnings. Every single thing Chloe ever made, a cut going back to the company that had tried to package her calm and sell it by the ounce.
Dave nodded. "How much is the debt again?"

"Forty-seven thousand, eight hundred and ninety-two dollars." She said it without hesitation. She'd memorized it the way you memorize a phone number you never wanted to call. "And my income is zero. Everything's frozen."

They sat there for a moment. The apartment was quiet—Maya was at her gallery shift, and the only sound was the ambient hum of Dave's recording equipment in the other room, the soft white noise of a life being documented that nobody was paying for.
"We could crowdfund," Dave said. He said it carefully, like he was setting something fragile on a table. "Your community would—"
Chloe shook her head. Once. Firm. The kind of head-shake that ended conversations.
"I'm not doing that."
"Chloe—"

"I'm not asking people to pay for my legal fight, Dave. That's—that's exactly what Serene does. *That's their whole model.* Find a community, extract value, call it empowerment. I'm not going to look at the people who found me when they were drowning and say, 'Hey, give me money so I can keep being your safe place.' That's grotesque."

Dave was quiet. He wanted to argue. She could see it in the set of his jaw, the way his fingers drummed his knee. But he also knew she was right, and that was worse than if she'd been wrong, because being right meant there was nothing to fight about.
"Okay," he said. "Okay."

He got up. Squeezed her shoulder once—his hand warm and solid and steady, the way it always was when he touched her—and went to the kitchen. She heard the cabinet open. The electric kettle. The small domestic sounds of someone making tea for someone else because there was nothing useful to say.
Chloe pulled the blanket back over her head.
She was still under there when Maya got home.

Maya didn't ask what was wrong. Maya had been on the internet today. Maya had seen the filing, the interview, the trending hashtag, the seventeen-member Discord server someone had already started called "Debra Wu Is A Corporate Demon And I Can Prove It With Charts." Maya had also seen Jordan's thread and had sent them a text that simply said "SLEEP" and had not received a response.

Maya went to her easel in the corner of the living room—she'd set it up there because the light was good and because it meant she could paint while still being in the same room as Chloe, which mattered to both of them in ways neither of them talked about.
She uncapped a tube of cerulean blue. Squeezed it onto the palette. Picked up a brush.

Under the blanket, Chloe listened to the sound of paint on canvas—the soft, rhythmic *shush-shush-shush* of it, the way it changed pitch when Maya loaded fresh color, the tiny catch in the stroke when she hit a texture she hadn't expected. It was familiar. Grounding. The auditory equivalent of a hand on a shoulder.
They stayed like that for a while. Chloe under the blanket. Maya at the easel. The apartment getting darker as the afternoon shifted into evening, neither of them reaching for a light.
Then Maya spoke. She didn't turn around. She didn't stop painting.
"Then we find another way."
Three words. Flat and certain, the way Maya said things when she'd already decided they were true and was just informing the universe of its obligations.

Chloe, under the blanket, listened to the brush keep moving—*shush, shush, shush*—and thought about the fact that Maya was currently painting what appeared to be a very large, very blue capybara floating in a sky full of clouds, and that this was the most Maya解决问题的 way possible: just paint the problem into something beautiful and dare reality to disagree.
Chloe didn't come out from under the blanket. Not yet.
But she did hum. Just once. Soft and low. The sound filled the room and then faded, like a candle making a decision about whether to stay lit.
Maya painted. The cerulean blue deepened into something closer to twilight.
Somewhere, Debra Wu was probably rehearsing her next interview.
Somewhere, Jordan was definitely not sleeping.
Somewhere, in a different apartment, a woman named Anika Patel was looking at a photo on her phone of a sleeping capybara and remembering what it meant to choose right.

And in this apartment, in this room, in this particular sliver of time, a capybara who used to be a PR manager and was now $47,892 in debt with no income and no legal leg to stand on lay under a blanket and listened to her best friend paint her into a sky full of clouds, and hoped—quietly, stubbornly, with the kind of dumb certainty that had gotten her this far—that another way existed.
It had to.
The universe owed her that much.
It probably didn't, actually. But she was going to pretend it did until it proved otherwise.
